Do you remember this slide from one of my earlier presentations? I call it my roadmap to success. At each step you don't know what you don't know, but the longer you are in business and the more successful you are, the more you become aware of what you don't know and what you might need going forward. Here's the thing. No matter how talented or intelligent you are, in order to grow you need people. If you employ or contract people you need process. If you don't build those processes you will spend the majority of your time managing the people you hire. Here's the test. If you take yourself out of the equation, would your business continue to grow and make money? Most small businesses are really just flexible jobs. If the owner stops working, the income stops or the business falls apart. I have owned too many businesses to believe in the fallacy that you can walk away from a business forever. Even Fortune 500 companies have boards and shareholders keeping an eye on them and trust me that's a good thing. The closer something is watched the less likely it is to break. When you're a small business processes can go a long way to keeping things in control. One of the skills you will need is the ability to map out your business and build processes. This applies to Fortune 500 companies all the way down to startups. I'm going to give you an example of two copywriters. If you're a consultant of any kind replace the word copywriter with whatever you do. Copywriter number one is called Sally. She is one of the most gifted and talented copywriters working today. Virtually every person who sees her work acknowledges that she has what it takes. As time goes by she progresses from writing Amazon product description and blog posts to writing ads and then full long copy sales pages and magologs. Her income goes from a few hundred dollars a month to twenty thousand dollars per sales page. The first problem she encounters is that she needs to constantly network to keep future jobs coming in. She has to talk with a lot of people on a daily basis. Some of them not so nice and one or two offend her delicate creative mind so much she can't write for hours after the conversation. When she does land a decent job she has to spend hours talking to the client just to keep them on board. She discovers you can only network your way to so much work before you exhaust those contacts. Eighty percent of her time is sourcing new jobs. The result is Sally works eighty to a hundred hours a week and is profoundly unhappy and feels under pressure all the time. Copywriter number two is called Jared. He is also talented but from the beginning he can see the ten thousand feet view. He realizes that scale will be difficult if he tries to do this himself and he doesn't want to deal with prospecting. He decides to focus on building the process first. He starts with the prospecting focus. He builds an avatar of the perfect client. How old he is, what niche he's in, how much money they make. Then he hires an outsourced person to contact people who meet his criteria. He puts them through a survey to make sure they are the right client for him. He then has the same outsourced person phone them to make sure they are a good fit for him to work with. That same outsourced person agrees the terms, conditions, deliverability and how much will be charged before passing on to Jared. Jared gets on a call with the client to make sure he has the creative part right. It's the only time he speaks with him. Both the client and Jared are happy because there's a timeline, there's a sense of control and everyone knows what to expect. Then a few months Jared's schedule is full. He increases his price and takes on a second full time copywriter. He also passes work on to Sally who he knows is talented. Three years later he has five copywriters working for him and makes millions of dollars a year and decides to move to the coast of France to study his other great passion of painting. Now it may seem a little simplistic and hard to believe that someone could do this but as you will see in the future lessons this is exactly what we do when we buy media or get clients for our other niches. So how do we build a process? Here's some examples first. This is an example of a social media campaign. This is our traffic process. With my companies all the processes are printed in a book so I can review them. My goal is to build a business based on process and procedures that make it easier for me to find someone who can run my business. They just need this book to take over the daily running of every task. This is the secret sauce that most businesses miss and why so many small businesses are constantly under pressure. Amazon's Jeff Bezos likes to say your margin is my opportunity. Without process and procedures you could never run a business like Amazon. A process should not be rigid. It should be fluid. It's more about an ongoing improvement than something that is set in stone. Once a month review any process you have and add, change or improve from your observations over that month. Step one, build the steps. When I design a process I like to make a checklist of all the stages. Then I like to map out those stages with images. To start with you might want to look at step shot or screen steps or text myths Camtasia. They're all good. Step two, map it out. Think about something you or your team does. Then try and map it out in logical steps. Just divide it into Lego type blocks of things. To start inexpensively you might want to look at Google draw from Google. Lucid charts from Lucid charts and Microsoft Word and Excel both can draw process maps. Step three, video it. This is the key to the success of your process. Create short videos that help explain how exactly you expect the process to work. I have used Jing, Snagit and Camtasia all from TechSmith. This is what inside my online companies look like with Microsoft Office for Business 365. We're made up into kind of websites, private websites in our team area and we have websites for copywriting. We take on new copywriters. We train them on boarding for new staff, support. All of the support procedures are in there. Affiliate marketing, launches, blogs, managers. Managers have a separate section, licenses that we issue or work with, consulting and consulting clients, media buys, team training and the team website. So it's all divided into separate websites and inside those are processes. So inside each section is a series of checklists. So if you go to one website and you go one layer down, you're going to see something like this. We have a checklist for new websites, new pages, pre-launch reports, sales pages. It depends what section you're looking at. So if you go one level down, you have a checklist that outlines what needs done. You might need to have a Fabricon. You might need titles and meta tags. You might need to cross browse the checks. You need, it needs to be proofread. All the links need to be checked. So there's a checklist there and who's responsible for it and if it's done or not. And there's also a link for the person who's implementing this. If they don't know how to do it, they can click a link and learn how that task is done. Then there's also the process is mapped out. And when it's mapped out, they can follow along the process with the checklist. After they've seen the checklist and they've seen it mapped out, there's also a video section. So the video section demonstrates how each step is done. Office 365 for business is a paid service, but for most people, Google Docs is good enough. And best of all, it's free. We used and still do use Google Docs for a lot of things. We have a process for our call team. We have a process for refunds. We have a process for customer support. We have a process for getting leads. You should be getting the picture by now. With every business, my dream is to build a money making machine. You can only do that with processes. Each day for one hour, the manager of my company will meet with the team. During the meeting, he is, one, getting feedback from the relevant team member that they are on schedule with their task. Two, he's bringing the team up to date with any new developments. Each team member should know and understand what and why they are doing. If you look at this image, you can see each team member has a sheet. Number one is a target. So what are they trying to achieve? They have to understand what the goal is. Number two, they plan it out individually. Number three, they have a timeline where they make milestones that can be measured. And number four, each task has a Gantt chart where it's a more granular view of when and at what time everything must be delivered. The manager should keep track visually of how each person is doing and what percentage of the time they are achieving your goals. We have a mini dashboard that the manager can look at each day. The more something is measured, the more it improves. In my own business, I can have up to 30 people working for me at any one time. Not one of those 30 people are even in the same country as me. I can only do this with carefully measured processes. This is an introduction to building processes. But if you want to design your own lifestyle and how much you make, processes is one of the keys to how to do that. That's all for now. Thanks for listening.
